summary: Asparouhova reflects on the exhausting nature of working in Silicon Valley's 'idea mines,' where the constant extraction and trading of thoughts transforms intellectual curiosity from leisure into labor. She describes her escapist fantasies of disappearing to Montana or working as a florist, but acknowledges she's unlikely to leave. As a coping mechanism, she has embraced 'being basic'—a deliberate rejection of intellectualism and constant deep thinking. She speculates that as idea production becomes associated with work rather than freedom, basic behavior might become a status symbol, signaling that one hasn't had to think for a living.
key_points: The creative economy transforms intellectual exploration from leisure into labor—'singing for one's supper' rather than genuine curiosity
Constant pressure to externalize thoughts prevents the slow, meandering process that produces genuinely original ideas
Being basic represents a deliberate information lobotomy—shutting off awareness of the deeper workings of the world
Basic, mediocre, and degenerate behaviors form a counter-counterculture pantheon resisting intellectualism, optimization, and moral authority respectively
Status signals may invert: just as tans shifted from marking laborers to leisure class, not-thinking may become more coveted than thinking
money_quotes: Silicon Valley is a coal mine for ideas.
We're like Scarlett O'Hara in Gone With the Wind: our gowns are impressively fashioned from velvet curtains, but our hands are still rough from the farm.
Sometimes it feels like I can't think in here, because people are constantly asking me to externalize my thoughts all the time.
Why would anyone want to have a deep, meaningful discussion all the time?
Being basic is a state of innocence and simplicity, Adam and Eve before the apple.
Being basic signals that I've shut my brain off. It's not quite like committing information suicide, but maybe it's like giving myself an information lobotomy.

If producing ideas becomes a symbol of work (having to think about stuff all day), rather than leisure (freedom to think about stuff all day), I wonder whether basic behavior will start to become covetable.
When I'm drinking mango White Claw and laughing in the dappled sunshine, I'm projecting a version of myself to the world that says, 'I haven't had to think a day in my life.'
key_insight: When intellectual work becomes mandatory labor rather than leisurely exploration, performative simplicity becomes the new status symbol—signaling freedom from having to think.
